div.h1: GPTQ Algorithm: Column-by-Column Quantization with Compensation

p.em: Step-by-step visualization of Hessian-aware weight quantization

g.text: Step 4: Recursive Update of Hessian Inverse
Key Insight: Compensation is What Makes INT4 Work

text.tspan: # Hessian: H = 2 * X^T X (from calibration data)
# Hessian: H = 2 * X^T X (from calibration data)
# Key insight: adjust remaining columns to minimize output error
# by adjusting weights that haven't been quantized yet
# Key insight: adjust remaining columns to minimize output error
# by adjusting weights that haven't been quantized yet
# After processing column j, update H^{-1} for remaining columns
# This maintains accuracy for subsequent column quantizations
# In practice, GPTQ often skips this for speed
# The initial H^{-1} approximation is usually sufficient
# After processing column j, update H^{-1} for remaining columns
# This maintains accuracy for subsequent column quantizations
# In practice, GPTQ often skips this for speed
# The initial H^{-1} approximation is usually sufficient

g.text: delta = W[:, i] - dequantize(Q[:, i], group_scale)

text.tspan: # Output: Quantized weights with minimal perplexity increase
# - zeros: Per-group zero points (usually 0 for symmetric)
# Output: Quantized weights with minimal perplexity increase
# - zeros: Per-group zero points (usually 0 for symmetric)

div.p: Naive INT4: Quantize all weights → error compounds → perplexity explodes (40+) GPTQ INT4: Quantize column → compute error → compensate in remaining columns → error absorbed The Hessian inverse tells us which weights matter most and how to adjust them to minimize output error. This is why GPTQ achieves perplexity increase < 1.0 while naive quantization fails catastrophically.
